description: A 12-lecture course teaching Python and software engineering fundamentals to kids. Each lecture includes a SE angle — a real software engineering concept tied to the Python topic.

lectures.title: Functions
lectures.intro: A function is a reusable block of code you give a name to. Instead of writing the same steps over and over, you write them once inside a function and call it whenever you need it.
lectures.se_angle: DRY principle — Don't Repeat Yourself. Functions are the first tool programmers use to avoid copy-pasting code. Every time you find yourself writing the same lines twice, that's a signal to extract a function.

sections.heading: Defining and Calling a Function
sections.concept: def keyword, function name, colon, indented body, calling with ()
sections.example:
def greet():
    print('Hello!')
    print('Nice to meet you.')

# Call the function — runs everything inside
greet()   # Hello! / Nice to meet you.
greet()   # called again — same output, no copy-paste!
sections.tip_text: The def line only defines the function — it doesn't run it yet. The code inside only runs when you call the function by writing its name followed by ().

sections.heading: Parameters and Arguments
sections.concept: passing values into a function via parameters
sections.example:
def greet(name):          # 'name' is a parameter
    print(f'Hello, {name}!')

greet('Alice')            # 'Alice' is the argument
greet('Bob')
# Hello, Alice!
# Hello, Bob!

def add(a, b):             # two parameters
    print(a + b)

add(3, 5)                 # 8
add(10, 20)               # 30

sections.heading: Return Values
sections.concept: return keyword, storing the result in a variable
sections.example:
def add(a, b):
    return a + b       # sends the result back to the caller

result = add(3, 5)    # store the returned value
print(result)         # 8
print(add(10, 20))    # 30 — use directly in print

# Without return, a function gives back None
def say_hi():
    print('Hi!')

value = say_hi()       # Hi! is printed
print(value)           # None
sections.tip_text: A function with return gives back a value you can store or use in calculations. A function without return gives back None — useful for functions that just print or do something, not compute something.

sections.heading: Default Parameter Values
sections.concept: default values make parameters optional
sections.example:
def greet(name='friend'):   # 'friend' is the default
    print(f'Hello, {name}!')

greet()            # Hello, friend!  (default used)
greet('Alice')     # Hello, Alice!   (default overridden)

def power(base, exp=2):     # exp defaults to 2
    return base ** exp

print(power(3))     # 9  — 3 squared
print(power(3, 3))  # 27 — 3 cubed

sections.heading: Variable Scope: Local vs Global
sections.concept: local scope — variables inside a function are invisible outside
sections.example:
def calculate():
    result = 42        # local variable — only exists inside
    print(result)      # 42 — works fine here

calculate()
# print(result)     # ERROR! result is not defined here

# Global variables (defined outside) are readable inside
pi = 3.14

def circle_area(r):
    return pi * r * r  # pi is global, we can read it

print(circle_area(5))  # 78.5
sections.tip_text: Variables created inside a function only exist there — you cannot use them outside. This is called local scope. It's a feature, not a bug: functions are self-contained so they don't accidentally mess with the rest of your program.

tasks.title: Hello, Function!
tasks.description: Write a function called say_hello() that prints "Hello, World!". Call it three times.
hints: Use def say_hello(): then indent the body with 4 spaces. Call it with say_hello().

tasks.title: Greeting with a Name
tasks.description: Write a function greet(name) that prints "Hello, [name]! Nice to meet you.". Call it with three different names.
hints: Define the function with one parameter: def greet(name):.
Inside the function use an f-string: print(f"Hello, {name}! Nice to meet you.").

tasks.title: Calculator Function
tasks.description: Write a function multiply(a, b) that returns the product of a and b. Print the result of calling it with several different pairs of numbers.
hints: Use return a * b inside the function, then print(multiply(3, 4)) outside.

tasks.title: Default Greeting
tasks.description: Modify greet so the name has a default value of "friend". Calling greet() with no argument should print "Hello, friend! Nice to meet you.".
hints: Set the default in the definition: def greet(name="friend"):.
Call it both ways: greet() and greet("Alice") to see the difference.

tasks.title: Temperature Converter ⭐
tasks.description: Write two functions: celsius_to_fahrenheit(c) and fahrenheit_to_celsius(f). Ask the user which direction they want to convert, read a temperature, call the right function, and print the result rounded to 1 decimal place.

lectures.title: Lists
lectures.intro: A list lets you store many values in one variable, in order. You can add, remove, and change items whenever you like.
lectures.se_angle: Arrays are the most fundamental data structure in software. Understanding lists unlocks almost every real-world program — from storing search results to processing game scores.

sections.heading: Creating and Indexing a List
sections.concept: square brackets, zero-based index, negative index
sections.example:
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

print(fruits[0])   # apple  — first item (index 0)
print(fruits[1])   # banana — second item
print(fruits[-1])  # cherry — last item (negative counts from end)
print(fruits[-2])  # banana

# Change an item
fruits[1] = 'mango'
print(fruits)  # ['apple', 'mango', 'cherry']
sections.tip_text: Python lists are zero-indexed: the first item is at index 0, not 1. This is true in almost every programming language — getting used to it now will save confusion later.

sections.heading: Slicing a List
sections.concept: list[start:stop], list[:3], list[2:], list[::2]
sections.example:
numbers = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]

print(numbers[1:3])   # [20, 30]  — index 1 up to (not including) 3
print(numbers[:3])    # [10, 20, 30] — from start up to index 3
print(numbers[2:])    # [30, 40, 50] — from index 2 to end
print(numbers[::2])   # [10, 30, 50] — every second item
print(numbers[::-1])  # [50, 40, 30, 20, 10] — reversed

sections.heading: Common List Methods
sections.concept: append, remove, pop, insert, sort, reverse, len
sections.example:
items = ['cat', 'dog', 'bird']

items.append('fish')       # add to end
print(items)  # ['cat', 'dog', 'bird', 'fish']

items.insert(1, 'hamster') # insert at index 1
print(items)  # ['cat', 'hamster', 'dog', 'bird', 'fish']

items.remove('dog')        # remove by value
print(items)  # ['cat', 'hamster', 'bird', 'fish']

last = items.pop()         # remove and return last item
print(last)   # fish

numbers = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
numbers.sort()             # sort in place (ascending)
print(numbers)  # [1, 1, 3, 4, 5]
print(len(numbers))  # 5

sections.heading: Looping Over a List
sections.concept: for item in list, enumerate, building a new list with a loop
sections.example:
scores = [85, 92, 78, 95, 60]

# Simple loop
for score in scores:
    print(score)

# enumerate() gives index and value together
for i, score in enumerate(scores):
    print(f'Student {i+1}: {score}')

# Build a new list using a loop
doubled = []
for score in scores:
    doubled.append(score * 2)
print(doubled)  # [170, 184, 156, 190, 120]

sections.heading: Checking Membership
sections.concept: in operator, not in, counting with count()
sections.example:
colors = ['red', 'green', 'blue']

if 'red' in colors:
    print('Red is in the list!')   # prints

if 'yellow' not in colors:
    print('Yellow is missing.')    # prints

# count() — how many times does a value appear?
numbers = [1, 2, 2, 3, 2, 4]
print(numbers.count(2))  # 3
sections.tip_text: Use if item in my_list to check whether a value exists — no loop needed. It's fast to write and easy to read, which is exactly what good code looks like.

tasks.title: My Playlist
tasks.description: Create a list of 5 song names. Print the first song, the last song, and the middle song using indexing. Then print the total number of songs.
hints: The first item is my_list[0]. The last is my_list[-1]. The middle of a 5-item list is index 2.

tasks.title: Shopping List Manager
tasks.description: Start with a list of 3 items. Use append() to add two more, remove() to delete one, then print the final list and its length.
hints: my_list.append('milk') adds to the end. my_list.remove('bread') deletes by value.
Use len(my_list) to get the count of items after all changes.

tasks.title: Sorted Scores
tasks.description: Create a list of 6 exam scores (numbers). Print them sorted from lowest to highest and then highest to lowest.
hints: my_list.sort() sorts in place ascending. my_list.sort(reverse=True) sorts descending. Be careful — sort() modifies the original list!

tasks.title: Find the Maximum
tasks.description: Without using Python's built-in max(), write a loop that finds the largest number in a list of 7 numbers you define. Print the result.
hints: Start with biggest = my_list[0]. Then loop through all items — if an item is greater than biggest, update biggest.
You already know if and for — combine them: if num > biggest: biggest = num.

tasks.title: Word Filter ⭐
tasks.description: Ask the user to enter 5 words one at a time (use a loop and append() to build the list). Then print only the words that are longer than 4 characters, each on its own line.

lectures.title: Dictionaries
lectures.intro: A dictionary stores data as key-value pairs — like a real dictionary where every word (key) has a definition (value). Keys must be unique; values can be anything.
lectures.se_angle: Key-value stores are everywhere in software: JSON configs, databases, HTTP headers, caches. Dictionaries are your first taste of structured data — the foundation of almost every real-world data model.

sections.heading: Creating a Dictionary
sections.concept: curly braces, key: value pairs, accessing by key
sections.example:
# Create a dictionary
person = {
    'name': 'Alice',
    'age': 12,
    'city': 'Kyiv'
}

# Access values by key
print(person['name'])   # Alice
print(person['age'])    # 12

# Add a new key
person['hobby'] = 'coding'
print(person)  # {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 12, 'city': 'Kyiv', 'hobby': 'coding'}

sections.heading: Safe Access with .get()
sections.concept: dict.get(key, default) — no crash if key is missing
sections.example:
scores = {'Alice': 95, 'Bob': 87}

# dict[key] crashes if key doesn't exist
# print(scores['Charlie'])  # KeyError!

# .get() returns None (or your default) instead
print(scores.get('Alice'))          # 95
print(scores.get('Charlie'))        # None — no crash
print(scores.get('Charlie', 0))     # 0   — custom default
sections.tip_text: Use dict.get(key) instead of dict[key] when the key might not exist — it returns None instead of crashing your program. In real applications, missing keys are common and must be handled gracefully.

sections.heading: Updating and Removing
sections.concept: dict[key] = new_value, del dict[key], dict.pop(key)
sections.example:
student = {'name': 'Bob', 'grade': 7, 'score': 80}

# Update an existing value
student['score'] = 95
print(student['score'])  # 95

# Delete a key
del student['grade']
print(student)  # {'name': 'Bob', 'score': 95}

# pop() removes and returns the value
removed = student.pop('score')
print(removed)  # 95
print(student)  # {'name': 'Bob'}

sections.heading: Looping Over a Dictionary
sections.concept: .keys(), .values(), .items() with for k, v in

sections.heading: Nested Dictionaries
sections.concept: a dict whose values are dicts, accessing nested keys
sections.tip_text: Nested dicts model real-world objects naturally: a person dict can have an "address" key whose value is another dict with "city" and "street". This is exactly the structure you'll see in JSON data from APIs.

tasks.title: My Profile
tasks.description: Create a dictionary with keys "name", "age", "city", and "hobby". Print each key and value on its own line using a loop.
hints: Loop over the dict with for key, value in my_dict.items(): and print both in an f-string.

tasks.title: Word Counter
tasks.description: Given the list ["apple", "banana", "apple", "cherry", "banana", "apple"], build a dictionary that counts how many times each word appears. Print the result.
hints: For each word, check if it's already in the dict. If yes, add 1; if no, set it to 1.
Use counts.get(word, 0) + 1 to get the current count (defaulting to 0) and add 1 in one step.

tasks.title: Phonebook
tasks.description: Create a phonebook dict with 3 name-to-number entries. Ask the user to enter a name; print their number or "Not found" if the name is missing.
hints: phonebook.get(name, 'Not found') returns the value or 'Not found' in one step — no if needed.

tasks.title: Update the Scores
tasks.description: Start with a dict of 4 students and their scores. Ask the user for a student name and a new score; update the dict and print all scores sorted by name.
hints: Check if the name exists with if name in scores before updating. Print a message if not found.
To print sorted by key: for k in sorted(scores): iterates alphabetically.

tasks.title: Nested Contact Book ⭐
tasks.description: Build a dict of 3 contacts where each value is itself a dict with keys "phone" and "email". Ask the user for a name and print that contact's full info. Handle the case where the name is not found.

lectures.title: Strings In Depth
lectures.page_title: Lecture 7: Strings In Depth — Python Lessons
lectures.intro: You have used strings since Lecture 1. Now we go deeper: slicing, searching, splitting, and formatting — tools that real programs use every day to process text.
lectures.se_angle: String manipulation is used in almost every program: parsing user input, formatting output, reading files, validating data. Mastering it early is one of the highest-leverage skills in programming.

sections.heading: Indexing and Slicing Strings
sections.concept: s[i], s[start:stop], s[::-1] to reverse — strings work like read-only lists

sections.heading: Useful String Methods
sections.concept: strip, split, join, replace, find, startswith, endswith, count

sections.heading: Checking String Contents
sections.concept: isdigit(), isalpha(), isalnum(), isupper(), islower()

sections.heading: Formatting Numbers in Strings
sections.concept: f"{value:.2f}" for decimals, f"{value:>10}" for alignment

sections.heading: Multiline Strings and Escape Characters
sections.concept: triple quotes, \n, \t, raw strings r"..."
sections.tip_text: Use a raw string r"C:\Users\name" when you need backslashes and don't want them treated as escape characters — very useful for file paths on Windows.

tasks.title: Reverse a Word
tasks.description: Ask the user for a word and print it reversed using slicing. Also print whether it's a palindrome (reads the same forwards and backwards).
hints: Reversed string: word[::-1]. A palindrome: word == word[::-1] — compare ignoring case with word.lower().

tasks.title: CSV Line Parser
tasks.description: Given the string "Alice,12,Kyiv,coding", split it by comma and print each field on its own line with a label: Name, Age, City, Hobby.
hints: text.split(',') returns a list of parts. Unpack with: name, age, city, hobby = parts.

tasks.title: Input Validator
tasks.description: Ask the user for their age. Keep asking until they enter something that contains only digits. Then convert it to an integer and print a confirmation message.
hints: Use input_str.isdigit() to check if all characters are digits.
Put the input inside a while True loop and break when valid.

tasks.title: Format a Receipt
tasks.description: Given a list of (item, price) tuples (at least 4 items), print a receipt where each item name is left-aligned in 20 characters and the price is right-aligned with 2 decimal places.
hints: Use f'{name:<20}{price:>8.2f}' for aligned columns.
Create the list like: items = [('Apples', 1.5), ('Bread', 2.3)]. Loop with for name, price in items:.

tasks.title: Password Validator ⭐
tasks.description: Ask the user for a password. Print whether it passes all rules: at least 8 characters, contains at least one digit, one uppercase letter, and one lowercase letter. For each failed rule, print a specific message.

lectures.title: Files
lectures.intro: Programs often need to save data between runs or read data created by another program. In this lecture you will learn how to open, read, write, and close files in Python.
lectures.se_angle: Persistence is a core software concept. Understanding file I/O is the gateway to databases, config files, and data processing pipelines. The patterns you learn here apply to every language.

sections.heading: Opening Files with the with Statement
sections.concept: open(), the with statement (context manager), modes 'r' 'w' 'a'
sections.tip_text: Always use with open(...): — it automatically closes the file even if an error occurs inside the block. This prevents data loss and resource leaks.

sections.heading: Reading a File
sections.concept: read(), readlines(), iterating line by line with for

sections.heading: Writing to a File
sections.concept: write mode 'w' overwrites, append mode 'a' adds to end, writelines()

sections.heading: Working with CSV Data
sections.concept: splitting lines by comma, building a list of dicts from rows

sections.heading: Handling File Errors
sections.concept: try/except FileNotFoundError, os.path.exists()

tasks.title: Note Saver
tasks.description: Ask the user to type a note. Save it to note.txt. Then read it back and print it.
hints: with open('note.txt', 'w') as f: f.write(text) to write. with open('note.txt', 'r') as f: print(f.read()) to read.

tasks.title: Line Counter
tasks.description: Ask the user for a filename. Try to open and count its lines. If the file doesn't exist, print a friendly error instead of crashing.
hints: lines = f.readlines() returns a list of lines. len(lines) is the count.
Wrap the open() in a try/except FileNotFoundError.

tasks.title: Diary
tasks.description: Each time the program runs, ask the user for a diary entry. Append it to diary.txt with today's date. At the end, read and print all entries.
hints: Use mode 'a' (append) so previous entries are not deleted.
Import datetime: from datetime import date. Get today: date.today().

tasks.title: CSV Reader
tasks.description: Create a text file students.csv with 3 lines in the format name,age,city. Read it in your program and print each row as a formatted sentence.
hints: line.strip().split(',') removes the newline and splits by comma.
Unpack with: name, age, city = line.strip().split(',').

tasks.title: Word Frequency ⭐
tasks.description: Ask the user for a filename. Read it, split into words, and count how many times each word appears (case-insensitive). Print the top 5 most frequent words and their counts.

lectures.title: Error Handling
lectures.intro: Programs crash when something unexpected happens — the user types a letter instead of a number, a file is missing, or you divide by zero. Error handling lets you catch those problems and respond gracefully instead of crashing.
lectures.se_angle: Defensive programming and graceful degradation. Real software always handles failure paths — this is what separates a toy script from production code. Unhandled exceptions are one of the most common causes of bugs reported by users.

sections.heading: What is an Exception?
sections.concept: runtime errors vs syntax errors, the traceback, common built-in exceptions

sections.heading: try / except
sections.concept: catching a specific exception type, else clause runs if no exception

sections.heading: Multiple except Clauses
sections.concept: ValueError, ZeroDivisionError, FileNotFoundError, catching multiple types

sections.heading: finally
sections.concept: finally always runs whether or not an exception occurred — for cleanup
sections.tip_text: Use finally for cleanup code that must always run: closing a file, printing a summary, resetting a state. It runs even when an exception is not caught.

sections.heading: Raising Your Own Exceptions
sections.concept: raise ValueError('message'), when and why to raise explicitly

tasks.title: Safe Division
tasks.description: Ask the user for two numbers. Divide them and print the result. Catch ZeroDivisionError and ValueError (non-numeric input) separately, printing a helpful message for each.
hints: Two separate except clauses: except ZeroDivisionError: and except ValueError:.
The conversion int(input(...)) raises ValueError if the user types letters.

tasks.title: Safe File Reader
tasks.description: Ask the user for a filename. Try to open and print it. If it doesn't exist, print "Sorry, that file was not found." instead of crashing.
hints: Use except FileNotFoundError: to catch missing files.

tasks.title: Retry Loop
tasks.description: Ask the user for a positive integer. Keep asking until they enter a valid one (use a while True loop + try/except). Then print the number doubled.
hints: Inside the loop: try to convert input to int. If it succeeds, check n > 0 and break. If ValueError, print a message and loop again.
Only break out of the loop after you've confirmed the input is a valid positive integer.

tasks.title: Validate Age
tasks.description: Write a function validate_age(age) that raises a ValueError with a descriptive message if age is negative or over 150. Call it inside a try/except and print the error message if it raises.
hints: raise ValueError('Age cannot be negative') inside the function.
Catch it with except ValueError as e: print(e) — e is the error message.

tasks.title: Safe Calculator ⭐
tasks.description: Build a 4-operation calculator (+, -, *, /). Ask the user for two numbers and an operator. Handle every possible error: non-numeric input, division by zero, unknown operator. Print a specific message for each problem.

lectures.title: Modules & the Standard Library
lectures.page_title: Lecture 10: Modules & the Standard Library — Python Lessons
lectures.intro: Python comes with hundreds of built-in modules — ready-made code you can import and use. You don't have to write everything from scratch.
lectures.se_angle: Standing on the shoulders of giants. Using the standard library is a core engineering skill: avoid reinventing the wheel, read documentation, compose existing tools. This mindset is how professional developers stay productive.

sections.heading: import and from … import
sections.concept: import math, from random import randint, aliasing with as

sections.heading: The math Module
sections.concept: math.sqrt, math.pi, math.floor, math.ceil, math.pow

sections.heading: The random Module
sections.concept: random.randint, random.choice, random.shuffle, random.random

sections.heading: The datetime Module
sections.concept: date.today(), strftime formatting, timedelta for date arithmetic

sections.heading: Writing Your Own Module
sections.concept: any .py file is a module, import it, if __name__ == '__main__'
sections.tip_text: Any Python file is a module. When you put reusable functions in their own file and import them, you are doing exactly what professional developers do every day. This is how large programs are organized into manageable pieces.

tasks.title: Circle Calculator
tasks.description: Ask the user for a radius. Use math.pi to calculate and print the area and circumference, each rounded to 2 decimal places.
hints: Area = π × r². Circumference = 2 × π × r. Use round(value, 2) or f'{value:.2f}'.

tasks.title: Lucky Number
tasks.description: Use random.randint(1, 100) to generate a secret number. Let the user guess it with a while loop, printing "Too high", "Too low", or "Correct!" after each guess. Count the guesses.
hints: From Lecture 3 you know the guessing loop pattern. Now replace the hardcoded secret with random.randint(1, 100).

tasks.title: Days Until Birthday
tasks.description: Ask the user for their birth day and month (as numbers). Use datetime to calculate how many days until their next birthday and print the result.
hints: from datetime import date. Get today with date.today(). Build the birthday date with date(year, month, day). Subtract dates to get a timedelta; use .days to get the number.

tasks.title: Shuffle a List
tasks.description: Create a list of 8 items (e.g., student names). Use random.shuffle() to randomize the order, then print the shuffled list. Repeat 3 times to show it's different each time.
hints: random.shuffle(my_list) modifies the list in place — no need to assign the result.

tasks.title: My Utils Module ⭐
tasks.description: Create a file my_utils.py with at least 3 useful functions (e.g. clamp(value, lo, hi), is_palindrome(s), celsius_to_fahrenheit(c)). In a separate main.py, import and test each function.

lectures.title: Classes & Objects
lectures.intro: So far you have used functions to organize code. Classes let you bundle data and behaviour together into a blueprint. An object is one instance created from that blueprint.
lectures.se_angle: Object-Oriented Programming is the dominant paradigm for large codebases. Understanding classes, encapsulation, and methods is essential for reading and writing professional Python. Every library you will ever use is built with classes.

sections.heading: Defining a Class and __init__
sections.concept: class keyword, __init__ constructor, self, instance attributes
sections.tip_text: self refers to the specific object being created or used. It is always the first parameter of any method, but you never pass it manually — Python passes it automatically.

sections.heading: Instance Methods
sections.concept: defining methods, calling them on an object, self.attribute inside methods

sections.heading: The __str__ Method
sections.concept: __str__ for human-readable representation, what print(obj) shows

sections.heading: Creating Multiple Objects
sections.concept: each instance has its own attributes, list of objects, calling methods in a loop

sections.heading: Inheritance
sections.concept: class Dog(Animal), super().__init__(), overriding methods, polymorphism preview
sections.tip_text: Inheritance means a child class gets all the methods of its parent for free, and can override or add new ones. This avoids duplicating code across similar classes — the DRY principle you learned in Lecture 4, applied to classes.

tasks.title: Student Class
tasks.description: Define a class Student with attributes name and grade. Add a method introduce() that prints a sentence like "Hi, I'm Alice and I'm in grade 7.". Create 3 Student objects and call introduce() on each.
hints: In __init__: self.name = name and self.grade = grade.
In introduce(): use self.name and self.grade in an f-string.

tasks.title: Rectangle Class
tasks.description: Define a class Rectangle with width and height. Add methods area() and perimeter(). Add __str__ so print(r) shows something like "Rectangle 4×6".
hints: def area(self): return self.width * self.height.
def __str__(self): return f'Rectangle {self.width}×{self.height}'.

tasks.title: Bank Account
tasks.description: Define a class BankAccount with a balance attribute (starting at 0) and methods deposit(amount) and withdraw(amount). Withdraw should print an error if there are not enough funds. Print the balance after each operation.
hints: In withdraw: if amount > self.balance: print('Not enough funds!') else subtract.
Start with self.balance = 0 in __init__. Each deposit/withdraw updates it.

tasks.title: List of Objects
tasks.description: Create a list of 5 Student objects. Write a function top_student(students) that takes the list and returns the student with the highest grade. Print their name.
hints: Loop through the list keeping track of the student with the highest grade — similar to the max-finding loop from Lecture 5.
Access an object's attribute: student.grade.

tasks.title: Animal Hierarchy ⭐
tasks.description: Create a base class Animal with attributes name and a method speak() that prints "[name] says ...". Create subclasses Dog and Cat that override speak() with the correct sound. Create a mixed list of animals and call speak() on each.

lectures.title: Final Project
lectures.intro: You have learned variables, conditions, loops, functions, lists, dictionaries, strings, files, error handling, modules, and classes. Now it's time to put it all together in a real project.
lectures.se_angle: Software projects require planning, incremental implementation, and testing. We introduce the concept of a project spec, milestones, and iterative development — the skills that distinguish a programmer from a software engineer.

sections.heading: What Makes a Good Project?
sections.concept: clear goal, defined inputs/outputs, broken into functions and maybe classes

sections.heading: Project Ideas
sections.concept: contact book CLI, quiz game, text adventure, budget tracker, password manager

sections.heading: Writing a Project Spec
sections.concept: what it does, what data it stores, what functions it needs, what errors to handle

sections.heading: Incremental Development
sections.concept: start small, add features one at a time, test each part before moving on

sections.heading: Code Review Checklist
sections.concept: readable names, no repeated code, handles errors, has comments on tricky parts
sections.tip_text: Before calling a project done, read your own code as if you're seeing it for the first time. Would a classmate understand it? Good code communicates intent, not just instructions to the computer.

tasks.title: Choose and Spec Your Project
tasks.description: Write a plain-text spec for your project: what it does, what data it uses, what the user can do (menu options), and at least 5 functions it will need.
hints: Think about a program you would actually want to use. A contact book, a quiz, a shopping list, a game score tracker.
Your spec doesn't have to be perfect — write it, then refine it as you build.

tasks.title: Build the Data Layer
tasks.description: Implement the data structures your project needs (lists, dicts, or classes) and write functions to add, remove, and find items. Test each function with a few print calls.
hints: Start with just one operation (e.g., add a contact). Get it working before adding more.
Use a list of dicts or a list of objects — whichever matches your project better.

tasks.title: Build the Menu Loop
tasks.description: Implement a while True menu loop that shows options and calls the right function for each. Include a "Quit" option that breaks the loop.
hints: Print numbered options, read the user's choice, use if/elif to dispatch.
Wrap each option's logic in its own function so the menu loop stays short and readable.

tasks.title: Add File Persistence
tasks.description: Save your data to a file when the user quits and load it when the program starts. Use a simple text or CSV format.
hints: On startup: if the file exists, read it and rebuild your data structure. If not, start empty.
On quit: write all current data to the file, one item per line.

tasks.title: Polish and Present ⭐
tasks.description: Add error handling to every user input. Write a one-line comment above every function explaining what it does. Run through all menu options and fix any bugs. Demo your program to a classmate or family member.

homeworks.title: Functions
homeworks.intro: Complete all tasks below. Write your code in homework_5.py and run it to check your answers.
homeworks.tip: Functions let you test each piece of your program in isolation. Write one function at a time and test it before moving on — this is how professionals work.

tasks.title: Even or Odd Checker
tasks.description: Write a function is_even(n) that returns True if n is even, False otherwise. Ask the user for 5 numbers using a loop, call the function for each, and print "[n] is even" or "[n] is odd".
hints: Use n % 2 == 0 to check if a number is even.
Collect the 5 numbers with for i in range(5) and input() inside.

tasks.title: FizzBuzz Function
tasks.description: Write a function fizzbuzz(n) that returns "Fizz" if n is divisible by 3, "Buzz" if divisible by 5, "FizzBuzz" if both, and the number as a string otherwise. Call it for 1–30 and print each result.
hints: Check n % 15 == 0 first (catches the both-divisible case), then 3, then 5.
Return str(n) for the fallthrough case so all branches return a string.

tasks.title: Mini Statistics
tasks.description: Write three functions: my_min(lst), my_max(lst), and my_average(lst) — each takes a list of numbers. Do not use Python's built-in min(), max(), or sum(). Test with two different lists.
hints: For min/max, initialise with the first item: result = lst[0]. Loop through the rest comparing.
For average: use your own summing loop, then divide by len(lst).

tasks.title: Recursive Countdown
tasks.description: Write a function countdown(n) that prints numbers from n down to 1 and then prints "Go!" — using recursion (the function calls itself) instead of a loop.
hints: Base case: if n == 0: print('Go!'); return.
Recursive case: print n, then call countdown(n - 1).

tasks.title: Text Analyser ⭐
tasks.description: Write a function analyze(text) that accepts a string and returns a dictionary with keys: "words" (word count), "chars" (character count excluding spaces), "sentences" (count of . ? ! characters), and "longest_word". Test it on at least two different strings and print the results.
